faq.question: What time is it in Singapore right now?
faq.answer: Singapore uses Singapore Standard Time (SST, UTC+8) year-round. Singapore does not observe Daylight Saving Time. The IANA identifier is Asia/Singapore. Singapore operates on Singapore Standard Time (SST), which is UTC+8 year-round. Unlike most major financial centers, Singapore never observes daylight saving time, meaning the clock never changes—9:00 AM SGT in January is exactly 9:00 AM SGT in July. As Southeast Asia's premier financial hub, Singapore's stable and predictable timezone makes it an ideal coordination anchor for ASEAN business operations spanning 11 nations and 670 million people.

faq.question: What time zone is Singapore in?
faq.answer: Singapore is in the Singapore Standard Time (SST) zone, permanently set at UTC+8. This is the same offset as China Standard Time, Philippine Standard Time, and Western Australian Standard Time. Singapore adopted UTC+8 in 1982 after briefly using UTC+7:30, aligning itself with Hong Kong and China to facilitate trade. Because Singapore never shifts for daylight saving, its UTC+8 offset is constant 365 days per year, simplifying scheduling with counterparts worldwide.

faq.question: Does Singapore observe daylight saving time?
faq.answer: No. Singapore does not observe daylight saving time and has never done so since adopting its current UTC+8 offset in 1982. This means Singapore Standard Time (SST) remains fixed at UTC+8 regardless of the season. For international business, this is a significant advantage: the time difference between Singapore and cities in Europe, the US, and Australia may shift by an hour when those regions change clocks, but Singapore's side of the equation never changes.

faq.question: What is the time difference between Singapore and New York?
faq.answer: Singapore is 13 hours ahead of New York during Eastern Standard Time (EST, UTC-5) in winter, and 12 hours ahead during Eastern Daylight Time (EDT, UTC-4) in summer. Because Singapore does not observe daylight saving time, the gap narrows when New York springs forward in March and widens again when New York falls back in November. The best overlap window for business calls is 8:00-11:00 PM EST (9:00 AM-noon SGT the following day in Singapore).

faq.question: What is the time difference between Singapore and London?
faq.answer: Singapore is 8 hours ahead of London during GMT (UTC+0) in winter, and 7 hours ahead during British Summer Time (BST, UTC+1) in summer. Since Singapore never changes its clocks, it is London that shifts, shrinking the gap from 8 to 7 hours between late March and late October. For business coordination, London callers should contact Singapore between 1:00 AM and 9:00 AM GMT to reach colleagues during standard Singapore business hours (9:00 AM-5:00 PM SGT).

faq.question: What time does the Singapore Exchange (SGX) open?
faq.answer: The Singapore Exchange (SGX) opens its securities market at 9:00 AM Singapore Standard Time (SGT) and closes at 5:30 PM SGT, Monday through Friday. A midday break previously observed until 2011 has since been eliminated, making SGX a continuous trading day. SGX is one of Asia's leading exchanges and serves as a gateway for international investors into ASEAN markets. Because SGT is UTC+8, SGX trading overlaps with the tail end of European trading days and opens well before New York's NYSE.

faq.question: What is the best time to call Singapore from the US or UK?
faq.answer: From the US East Coast, the optimal window to call Singapore during business hours is 8:00 PM-11:00 PM EST in winter (or 7:00 PM-10:00 PM EDT in summer), which corresponds to 9:00 AM-noon SGT. From the US West Coast, call between 5:00-8:00 PM PST. From London, the window is 1:00 AM-9:00 AM GMT (winter) or midnight-8:00 AM BST (summer) to reach Singapore's 9:00 AM-5:00 PM business hours—making early-morning London calls the practical solution for same-day Singapore responses.

faq.question: What are typical business hours in Singapore?
faq.answer: Standard business hours in Singapore run Monday through Friday, 9:00 AM to 6:00 PM SGT. Many multinational corporations in the Central Business District (CBD) operate 9:00 AM to 5:30 PM. Singapore's multicultural workforce celebrates public holidays for Chinese New Year, Deepavali, Hari Raya, and Christmas, totaling 11 public holidays annually. The city-state's efficient public transit system means workers often arrive early; morning meetings starting at 8:30 AM are common in the finance and tech sectors clustered around Marina Bay and One-North.

seo_description: Current time in Singapore. Live SGT clock, UTC+8 offset, no daylight saving time, and time differences with New York, London, Tokyo, and Sydney.

content_blocks.title: Singapore Time Zone Explained: Singapore Standard Time (UTC+8)
content_blocks.content: Singapore operates on Singapore Standard Time (SST), a fixed offset of UTC+8 that never changes throughout the year. This makes Singapore one of the most timezone-predictable major cities on the planet—there is no daylight saving adjustment to account for, no spring-forward in March, no fall-back in October. The city-state adopted its current UTC+8 offset on January 1, 1982, aligning with Hong Kong, China, and the Philippines to streamline trade and finance across Greater Asia. Prior to 1982, Singapore briefly used UTC+7:30, an offset inherited from its Malaysian federation years. Today, UTC+8 places Singapore at the heart of the Asia-Pacific business day: when Tokyo (UTC+9) is at 10:00 AM, Singapore is at 9:00 AM; when Dubai (UTC+4) is at 5:00 AM, Singapore is at 9:00 AM. This positioning lets Singapore serve as a natural scheduling bridge between East Asia and the Middle East. For teams coordinating with Singapore from London, the gap is 8 hours in winter (GMT) and 7 hours in summer (BST)—a shift driven entirely by the UK changing its clocks, not Singapore. Similarly, the Singapore-New York time difference ranges from 12 to 13 hours depending on US daylight saving. Use WhatTime.city's time converter to instantly calculate the current Singapore time from any city in the world, accounting for all seasonal DST changes in other regions.

content_blocks.title: Singapore as a Global Business Hub: Finance, Shipping & Tech
content_blocks.content: Singapore punches far above its weight as a 728 km² city-state that ranks among the world's top financial centers alongside New York, London, and Hong Kong. The Singapore Exchange (SGX) opens at 9:00 AM SGT and closes at 5:30 PM SGT, operating a continuous session with no lunch break since 2011—a reform that placed SGX among the most efficient markets in Asia. SGX lists over 700 companies from 40 countries, making it the primary gateway for international capital entering ASEAN's collective $3.6 trillion economy. Beyond finance, Singapore is the world's second-busiest container port by tonnage, handling over 37 million TEUs annually through the Port of Singapore Authority (PSA). Shipping operations run 24/7, meaning port coordination calls can be placed at any Singapore time. The tech sector anchors itself at one-north, Singapore's innovation district housing Google, Meta, Stripe, and over 1,000 startups. Major tech firms follow standard 9:00 AM-6:00 PM SGT hours, though global engineering teams frequently stagger shifts to cover Singapore time. Singapore and Tokyo share only a 1-hour gap (SGT is UTC+8, JST is UTC+9), making the two cities natural partners for intra-Asia scheduling. For reaching Sydney from Singapore, the gap is 2 hours behind in standard time (AEST) or 3 hours behind during Australian Eastern Daylight Time (AEDT). Use WhatTime.city's meeting planner to find the best overlap windows for Singapore-centered global calls.

content_blocks.title: Calling Singapore from North America, Europe & the Middle East
content_blocks.content: Reaching Singapore from Western time zones requires early-evening effort from North America and middle-of-the-night commitment from Europe—but strategic scheduling makes it entirely manageable. From New York, the prime calling window is 8:00 PM-11:00 PM EST (winter) or 7:00 PM-10:00 PM EDT (summer), which lands on Singapore's 9:00 AM-noon business window the following calendar day. From Los Angeles, the Singapore-Los Angeles gap is 16 hours (PST) or 15 hours (PDT), meaning West Coast callers should dial between 5:00-8:00 PM PST to reach Singapore during core hours. From London, the 7-8 hour gap means any call placed after 1:00 AM GMT will hit Singapore's business day; practically, most London-Singapore calls happen via early-morning London shifts or late-afternoon Singapore flexibility. Dubai sits just 4 hours behind Singapore (UTC+4 vs UTC+8), making the Singapore-Dubai corridor one of the easiest international pairs to schedule—a 9:00 AM Singapore call reaches Dubai at 5:00 AM, while a noon Singapore call aligns with Dubai's 8:00 AM. From Berlin and Paris, Singapore is 7 hours ahead during CET (winter) and 6 hours ahead during CEST (summer), creating a narrow but workable overlap if European teams start early. Singapore's four official languages—English, Mandarin, Malay, and Tamil—mean that international callers typically find English-language business communication seamless. For precise, real-time scheduling across all these corridors, WhatTime.city's time converter handles all DST logic automatically.
